If you prefer to invest in individual stocks and do not mind devoting some regular time researching and monitoring your positions, then this route is appropriate. Also, there are some distinct advantages to owning individual stocks. The first one is that there are no ongoing fees. Even to buy or sell, most online brokers offer commission-free trades. On the contrary, most funds charge some ongoing fees that can add overtime and have to come from your total returns. The second advantage of owning individual funds is avoiding over-diversification. Sure, diversification is important to avoid risk, but it can quickly lead to over-diversification. We know that over-diversification usually leads to market average returns (minus the fund fees). With funds, especially a portfolio of funds, it is difficult (if not impossible) to avoid over-diversification and some duplication.

When you own individual stocks, you can decide how much you want to diversify. But, we recommend having at least 15 individual stocks to avoid a stock-specific risk.

Below, we will describe a simple method to structure and create a DGI portfolio.

Let's consider the following popular dividend ETFs and look at the top-10 holdings of each fund.

  1. Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD),
  2. Vanguard High Dividend Yield Index Fund ETF (VYM),
  3. Vanguard Dividend Appreciation Index Fund ETF (VIG),
  4. Fidelity High Dividend ETF (FDVV).

SCHD is one of the most popular dividend ETFs. VYM and VIG are two Vanguard dividend ETFs, the first one for higher dividends, while VIG has more growth names. Lastly, FDVV is the dividend fund from Fidelity.

Steps:

  1. Take the top 10 holdings of each of the above funds. In all, we will get 40 stocks.
  2. Sort and weed out the duplicate holdings. We are left with 28 stocks with an average dividend yield of roughly 2.60%. This is a good first list.
  3. Remove duplicates based on sector or industry segments, especially if there are too many names from one sector or segment. For example, we had five companies from the healthcare industry. Some level of subjective judgment is necessary in this step.
  4. Finally, we are left with 15 names. The average yield is 2.88%, and the past 10-year dividend growth is 11.77%.
  5. These 15 stocks are presented below.

(BAC), (KO), (HD), (CSCO), (MSFT), (BLK), (PFE), (ABBV), (UNH), (CVX), (AVGO), (V), (PG), (PM), and (VZ).

Table-1: DGI Bucket

(II) The High-Income Bucket: (35% of the assets)

Now, we know that the DGI bucket is going to provide us income somewhere in the range of 3% to 4%, but that may not be enough income for many folks. This bucket will invest some high-income funds that will aim for 7% to 8% income and elevate the overall income yield of the portfolio. These funds would be drawn mainly from three categories: CEFs (Closed-End funds), REITs (Real Estate Investment Trusts), and BDCs (Business Development Companies). In addition to the high-income yield, these funds would add a layer of diversification that we could not have achieved from the DGI bucket. This will add a number of new asset classes that may be missing if we only had the DGI bucket.

We selected the following 10 funds:- a combination of ETFs (Exchange-Traded funds), traditional mutual funds, and CEFs. One of the main criteria of selection is the past track record of the funds and the income yield that they provide:

(FAGIX), (ADX), (PEO), (AMLP), (JEPQ), (MPV), (RFI), (JFNIX), (BIZD), and (UTG).

Table-2: High-Income Bucket

Note: In the above table, there is one CEF that is trading at a significant premium (MPV at 17%). We would not recommend buying this at such a high premium, and it may be best to accumulate this in many installments rather than a lump sum (or wait until the premium gets smaller).

(III) The Hedging Bucket: (30% of the assets)

As such, this bucket is optional. The first two buckets (DGI and High-income) can provide an investor with more than enough diversification, growth, and income. However, there is one aspect that is missing from the first two buckets, and that is “hedging and downside protection.” For some investors, that is not important as they can tolerate the ups and downs of the market. But for many, especially retirees, it can be a big problem. Many times, some retirees just put aside enough cash for their 2–3 years of expenses, and that works well for them. But we also know that cash barely earns more than the rate of inflation. So, keeping a large amount of cash is probably not the best option. To address this issue, we will add a third bucket that is specifically designed to provide hedging and downside protection without incurring any ongoing costs.

For this bucket, we will have a pool of six securities, each representing a specific asset class. Based on a momentum formula, we will buy two securities every month (and hold them for the next month). The catch here is that these asset classes have low correlation among them and can move in different directions. For example, if the stocks are trending down, GOLD may be trending up or vice versa. All it means is that they are not co-related. This model tries to capture that and tries to stay in assets that are trending up and avoid the ones that are trending down. Also, if all other assets are trending down, the model tries to stay in cash.

For this bucket, we will select securities representing Equities, Gold, Real estate, Treasuries, Commodities, and Cash (parked in a money-market fund).

  1. ProShares Ultra QQQ ETF (QLD)
  2. SPDR Glod Shares ETF (GLD)
  3. iShares 20+ Year Treasury Bond ETF (TLT)
  4. Cohen & Steers Real-estate & Preferred Fund (RNP)
  5. Vanguard Materials ETF (VAW)
  6. Cash (or Money market fund).

Table-3: List of six securities:

Note: QLD is an ultra QQQ (Nasdaq-100), and it aims to provide 2x returns of QQQ after fees. We do not recommend QLD as a buy-and-hold investment, but this is inside the rotation portfolio, which means it will be held only during times when there is strong positive momentum. More conservative investors could replace QLD with QQQ (with expectations of lower returns).

2. Not all securities in this model provide significant income. Moreover, since we are not constantly invested

in any one security, we may miss the dividend payments. So, income investors who need to draw income should aim to withdraw up to 5% (annualized) income at the end of each year.

The monthly rotation process:

At the end of each month, we will calculate the performance (total returns, including any dividends or distributions) of all six securities over the last 3 months. We will then select the top two securities based on total performance. The capital in this hedging bucket is then invested in these two securities, divided equally (50% each) for the next month. This process is repeated every month.

Backtested Performance – Hedging/Rotational Bucket:

With the six securities that we have chosen, we can go back to January 2007, which covers a period of 18-plus years. For backtesting, obviously we want to cover many types of environments. The last 18 years do cover a few significant events, such as the 2008-2009 financial crisis, the 2014-2015 energy prices crash, the 2020 pandemic, the 2022 tech crash, as well as other factors like zero interest rates and high interest rates.

Chart-1: Growth of $100,000, Rotational Model vs. S&P 500

Table-4: Performance metrics (Model vs. S&P500)

Risks and potential concerns:

There are several risks and downsides that an investor should be aware of before investing in the strategy described above. First, there is always the market risk. After all, nearly 90% of investments are in stocks. Despite the hedging bucket (which covers only a third of the portfolio), our investments will be subject to downside risk if the overall market takes a dive. Second, due to the presence of a 'Rotation' bucket, it requires monthly rotations and some level of ongoing work. Any sort of inconsistency can reduce the potential of hedging. A rotation bucket would also add to tax complications unless it is performed inside a tax-deferred account (like an IRA account).

Concluding Thoughts:

At first glance, a portfolio with three unique buckets/strategies may appear to be a lot of work. But when you look at it, the first two buckets are simply 'buy-and-hold' with little work once they have been established. The third bucket (rotational) requires some work, but no more than an hour of work on a monthly basis. Furthermore, it provides the investor all the flexibility as to how much they want to allocate to each bucket based on their personal situation and goals. In addition, since the total returns from the buy-and-hold buckets will be subject to valuations at the time of buying, it is important to establish them over a period of time to avoid buying at or near the top.

The portfolio can comfortably provide a 5% to 6% income to retirees and income investors, market-matching total returns with lower volatility and drawdowns.

data.title: Nu Holdings: Deposit Surge, Net Interest Margins Squeeze. A Risky Balance
data.description:
This is my first coverage of Nu Holdings Ltd. (NYSE:NU). The stock emerged on my radar following the Q4 2024 panic selloff, which led to a 19% discount on its share price.

From a valuation perspective, even after the selloff, the company seems quite expensive if we consider P/B as the comparison metric. In fact, Nu is the most expensive company (by this metric) within the diversified banking industry.

In this article, I will discuss several factors that deterred me from buying the dip, among them a contraction in NIM despite a significant increase in deposits in Mexico and Colombia.

The stock price is at a critical point. If it drops below the long-term support level, I expect it to fall to the low $8 range. However, it's too soon to call this a sell. For now, it's best to hold.

The Downside

The company released Q4 and FY 2024 earnings results on February 20, after market close. I see several concerning drivers that led to the 19% selloff.

First, the company reported a contraction in NIM (net interest margins) for the third consecutive time since Q2 2024.

Out of the 70bps decline in Q4 2024, about 45% was due to unfavorable FX rates. A quick look at the chart below shows the dominance of the US Dollar over the Brazilian Real in FY 2024. However, note that since January 2025, there has been a significant pullback.

The next two drivers accounted for the remaining 55% of the NIM contraction, in roughly the same relative weight:

The remaining 55% of the contraction is largely equally explained by two things. Number one, the drop in yields in the overall credit portfolio in Brazil as we grow secured lending more than we grow unsecured lending in terms of loan balance. And two, as a result of the growing deposits in Mexico and Colombia, which are still paying kind of interest that are higher than the intrabank deposit rates. - Guilherme Lago, Q4 2024 earnings call.

The shift in the loan portfolio mix toward secured lending (i.e. payroll & FGTS-backed loans) carries a lower interest yield when compared to unsecured lending (i.e. personal and SME loans). To be specific, total secured lending grew by 615% yoy in 2024, totaling $1.4 billion in portfolio size. This is approximately 23% of the company's total lending portfolio.

In regard to the growing deposits in Mexico and Colombia, the company used high deposit rates as a customer acquisition strategy. Naturally, this leads to an increase in the cost of funds, which lowers interest spreads. To be specific, deposits in Mexico increased by 438% yoy, totaling $4.5 billion. In Colombia, deposits reached $1.3 billion in just two quarters.

The next disappointing result comes from a decline in the loan to deposit ratio of 1 percentage point qoq, totaling 39%. Total deposits increased by 55% yoy (on a FX neutral basis), while the lending portfolio only grew by 45% yoy. Why is this trend disappointing? Well, these deposits generate costs (interest paid on customer balances). If the bank is not using them to lend money, this can lead to net interest income being pressured. Until these deposits are (of course, not the entire amount) deployed into loans, NIM could remain compressed.

Another point to highlight during the earnings call was the strategic slowdown in PIX financing. Management confirmed PIX financing remains highly profitable and in strong demand, however, Nu is deliberately slowing down eligibility expansion for this product.

... we basically reduced the eligibility growth of PIX financing for the lowest kind of credit bands of financing, primarily to address what we saw some deteriorations in second-order impacts related primarily to NPS, churn, and engagement.

The company is now more selective with lower credit bands for PIX financing, which makes sense considering that credit loss allowances expenses increased by 12% qoq, totaling $804M in Q4 2024.

Related to this topic, I considered including below an excerpt from the Q&A.

Citi analyst Gustavo Schroden asked the following:

We've seen some signals of a potential deterioration in inflation, inflation for foods and we know that there is a high correlation between inflation and asset quality, also an environment of high interest rates, some questions about the macroeconomic environment, and we've seen mean the system or most of banks that we talk to adopting a more conservative approach on the credit concession... ... I'd like to understand what's the difference here, and if you do not have concerns about potential adverse selection in this environment.

COO Youssef Lahrech replied the following:

And so this is why we always say that when we underwrite a loan or a credit card or any form of credit, we expect the future to be significantly worse than the past, right? Those are the assumptions that we put in our models to underwrite.

Based on the comment above, I believe the increase in credit loss allowances is more proactive rather than the company being reactive to an increase in delinquencies. Nonetheless, this is something to keep an eye on in the next earnings call.

Finally, another factor to keep an eye on is the expansion towards higher-income customers (Ultravioleta credit product), who have lower APRs. Naturally, this negatively affects credit yields. To be specific, Ultravioleta credit purchase volume grew 106% yoy, accounting for 10% of total Brazilian credit card volume.

Is the slowdown in PIX financing of lower credit scores and the increase in Ultravioleta credit purchase volume an early indication of macro headwinds in the Brazilian economy? In my view, it could be, however, I believe it's too early to have certainty.

From a valuation perspective, the company has a FWD P/B ratio of 5.9. That's more than 3x of the financials sector median. In fact, if we look at the top diversified bank industry, Nu is the most expensive when looking at P/B.

The Upside

Despite possible macroeconomic uncertainties in the Brazilian economy, the company's NPL coverage is over 200%, which significantly mitigates default risks.

As I said before, the loan portfolio growth was up 45% yoy. Even though this was a lower increase than overall deposits (on a FX neutral basis), this high double-digit growth is something that I still see as positive. Additionally, I believe the increase in deposits, especially in Mexico and Colombia, could eventually lead to conversions into the loan portfolio later this year.

Another positive factor is the steady increase in ROE, despite the increase in secured lending.

Additionally, net margins seem to be quite healthy, despite a qoq decline in Q4 2024.

From a price action perspective, following the Q4 2024 selloff, the share price is trading close to a support trend line. I'll keep a close eye on this stock over the next few weeks, as a breakout below this support line could indicate a downturn in investor sentiment, which can lead to a further decline, down to $8 level (which seems to be the next historical support level).

Conclusion

To wrap up, I rate Nu Holdings as a hold. I'll be keeping a close eye on the share price over the next few weeks, and any news coming from Brazil regarding macroeconomic factors that could lead to an increase in delinquency rates. For the moment, the company's NPL coverage is over 200%, which is a positive sign. If the credit yield keeps declining over the next quarters, I believe there is a good chance to see the share price trading in the low $8s.
